note_data.title: Series
keyPoints: An **arithmetic series** has constant common difference d between consecutive terms, with nth term Un = a + (n-1)d
The sum of the first n terms of an arithmetic series is **Sn = n/2[2a + (n-1)d]** or **Sn = n/2(first term + last term)**
A **geometric series** has constant common ratio r between consecutive terms, with nth term Un = ar^(n-1)
The sum of the first n terms of a geometric series is **Sn = a(1-r^n)/(1-r)** when r ≠ 1, or **Sn = na** when r = 1
A geometric series converges to a **sum to infinity S∞ = a/(1-r)** if and only if **|r| < 1**
The **binomial series** expansion is **(1+x)^n = 1 + nx + n(n-1)x²/2! + n(n-1)(n-2)x³/3! + ...** valid when |x| < 1
For negative or fractional powers, the binomial series is an **infinite series** that converges only when **|x| < 1**
When finding the sum to infinity, first check that **|r| < 1**, otherwise the series diverges and has no finite sum

definitions.definition: A series where each term is obtained by adding a constant value (common difference) to the previous term
A series where each term is obtained by multiplying the previous term by a constant value (common ratio)
The value that an infinite geometric series approaches when |r| < 1, given by S∞ = a/(1-r)
The constant value r by which each term is multiplied to get the next term in a geometric sequence
An infinite series that approaches a finite limit as the number of terms approaches infinity

howToAnswer.instruction: Calculate the exact value using the appropriate formula and show all working steps clearly
Prove the given result by starting with known formulas and manipulating algebraically to reach the required expression
Find the value by setting up and solving an equation, clearly stating any conditions

modelAnswer.question: A geometric series has first term 8 and common ratio -1/2. Find (i) the 6th term, (ii) the sum of the first 10 terms, (iii) the sum to infinity.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Correct formula Un = ar^(n-1) and calculation of U6
Mark 2: Correct finite sum formula and substitution
Mark 5: Correct sum to infinity formula

commonMistakes: Never write S∞ without first checking |r| < 1 — always verify the convergence condition
Students often confuse arithmetic and geometric formulas — check whether you add or multiply to get the next term
Never use Sn = na for geometric series — this only applies when r = 1 exactly
Students forget to use brackets with negative ratios — (-1/2)^5 ≠ -1/2^5
Never assume a series converges — always state and verify the condition |r| < 1 for sum to infinity questions
examinerTips: Series questions appear in every Paper 1, usually worth 8-12 marks total
Always identify the type of series first before selecting the appropriate formula
Show the convergence check |r| < 1 explicitly for full marks in sum to infinity questions
In binomial expansion questions, state the validity condition |x| < 1 for full marks
formulas: Geometric: Un = ar^(n-1), Sn = a(1-r^n)/(1-r), S∞ = a/(1-r) when |r| < 1
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: No standard diagram required, but clearly label terms in sequence notation: U1, U2, U3, ... or a, a+d, a+2d, ... for arithmetic sequences
